diagrams.description: The evolution of microeconomics spans from the classical insights of Adam Smith (1776) and Ricardo, through the Marginal Revolution of the 1870s (Jevons, Menger, Walras) and Marshall’s Principles (1890). Game theory (von Neumann & Morgenstern, 1944; Nash, 1950) introduced strategic interaction, while information economics (Akerlof, 1970) explained market asymmetries. Behavioural economics (Kahneman & Tversky, 1979) challenged rational choice, enriching the discipline with psychological realism.

Classical economics 1817 : Ricardo’s Principles
Comparative advantage 1870s : Marginal Revolution
Jevons, Menger, Walras 1890 : Marshall’s Principles
Neoclassical synthesis 1944 : von Neumann & Morgenstern
Game Theory 1950 : Nash Equilibrium 1970 : Akerlof’s Market for Lemons
Information asymmetry 1979 : Kahneman & Tversky
Prospect theory, behavioural economics
